feed.title: Fong Lab
feed.subtitle: Harnessing plasticity for neural rehabilitation and lifelong learning

entry.title: Spring 2026 STEM workshop for visually-impaired youth

link.title: Spring 2026 STEM workshop for visually-impaired youth

entry.content: By popular demand, the Fong lab held a Pi Day STEM workshop for blind and visually-impaired youth featuring our activities building solar cars, conducting EMG recordings, and using neural prosthetics. We had a huge turnout, with Sydney, Danielle, Chao, Max, Audrey, Ming, Morgan (and Freya!), Woon Ju, Josh, Zimil, Sophia, and Raveena all helping students through the various activities. Weather was sunny so we were able to race our cars outside. Congratulations to our winner, Dimitrius! Thank you to GT CEISMC, CVI, GT INNS, and GT-Emory BME for the continued support!
entry.summary: By popular demand, the Fong lab held a Pi Day STEM workshop for blind and visually-impaired youth featuring our activities building solar cars, conducting EMG recordings, and using neural prosthetics. We had a huge turnout, with Sydney, Danielle, Chao, Max, Audrey, Ming, Morgan (and Freya!), Woon Ju, Josh, Zimil, Sophia, and Raveena all helping students through the various activities. Weather was sunny so we were able to race our cars outside. Congratulations to our winner, Dimitrius! Thank you to GT CEISMC, CVI, GT INNS, and GT-Emory BME for the continued support!

entry.title: Welcome Aanchal :)
entry.content: We are happy to welcome Aanchal Acharya to the Fong Lab! Aanchal is a first year Neuroscience undergraduate at Georgia Tech. She plans to pursue medical school and hopes to become a neurosurgeon focused on treating neurological disorders and reducing stigma around neurodivergence. She will be conducting research with Max on studying how temporary visual deprivation affects auditory and visual cortical plasticity in adult mice. Welcome Aanchal!
entry.summary: We are happy to welcome Aanchal Acharya to the Fong Lab! Aanchal is a first year Neuroscience undergraduate at Georgia Tech. She plans to pursue medical school and hopes to become a neurosurgeon focused on treating neurological disorders and reducing stigma around neurodivergence. She will be conducting research with Max on studying how temporary visual deprivation affects auditory and visual cortical plasticity in adult mice. Welcome Aanchal!

entry.title: Welcome Audrey :)
entry.content: We are pleased to welcome Audrey Garlick to the Fong Lab! Audrey is a first-year undergraduate studying Neuroscience at Georgia Tech with broad interests in learning, plasticity, and neurological disease. She is particularly interested in neurodegenerative disorders and hopes to pursue an MD-PhD in the future. In the lab, Audrey will study how optogenetic manipulation of neural circuits drives thalamocortical plasticity in mice under the mentorship of PhD candidate Yichao Zhao. Welcome, Audrey!
entry.summary: We are pleased to welcome Audrey Garlick to the Fong Lab! Audrey is a first-year undergraduate studying Neuroscience at Georgia Tech with broad interests in learning, plasticity, and neurological disease. She is particularly interested in neurodegenerative disorders and hopes to pursue an MD-PhD in the future. In the lab, Audrey will study how optogenetic manipulation of neural circuits drives thalamocortical plasticity in mice under the mentorship of PhD candidate Yichao Zhao. Welcome, Audrey!

entry.title: Welcome Sophie :)
entry.content: We’re excited to welcome Sophie Medico to the lab as a new research technician. Sophie joins the lab with an BS in Neuroscience from Baylor, and a year and a half of experience doing calcium imaging, behavior, and anatomy work in mouse cerebellum and cortex. We are excited to have her join our team. Welcome, Sophie!
entry.summary: We’re excited to welcome Sophie Medico to the lab as a new research technician. Sophie joins the lab with an BS in Neuroscience from Baylor, and a year and a half of experience doing calcium imaging, behavior, and anatomy work in mouse cerebellum and cortex. We are excited to have her join our team. Welcome, Sophie!

entry.title: STEM workshop for visually-impaired youth
entry.content: The Fong lab organized a STEM workshop for blind and visually-impaired youth to learn about robotics and neural prosthetics, with support from the GT Center for Education Integrating Science, Mathematics, and Computing (CEISMC), the Atlanta Center for Visually Impaired (CVI), the GT-Emory Department of Biomedical Engineering, and the GT Institute for Neuroscience, Neurotechnology, and Society (INNS). We reprised our successful solar car activity. Sydney gains developed a new module for the workshop focused on recording and listening to muscle activity using EMG electrodes and using these signals to control a robotic arm. Sydney, Eli, Danielle, Chao, Max, and Ming all participated as mentors. Although it was a cloudy day and students couldn’t race their cars outside using their solar panels, they swapped out the power source for batteries and raced their cars through the second floor hallways of Clough Commons! Congrats to Sidney for winning the race!
entry.summary: The Fong lab organized a STEM workshop for blind and visually-impaired youth to learn about robotics and neural prosthetics, with support from the GT Center for Education Integrating Science, Mathematics, and Computing (CEISMC), the Atlanta Center for Visually Impaired (CVI), the GT-Emory Department of Biomedical Engineering, and the GT Institute for Neuroscience, Neurotechnology, and Society (INNS). We reprised our successful solar car activity. Sydney gains developed a new module for the workshop focused on recording and listening to muscle activity using EMG electrodes and using these signals to control a robotic arm. Sydney, Eli, Danielle, Chao, Max, and Ming all participated as mentors. Although it was a cloudy day and students couldn’t race their cars outside using their solar panels, they swapped out the power source for batteries and raced their cars through the second floor hallways of Clough Commons! Congrats to Sidney for winning the race!

entry.title: Ming wins SFN Next Generation Award

link.title: Ming wins SFN Next Generation Award

entry.content: Ming received the 2025 Next Generation Award from the Society for Neuroscience in honor of her efforts in science education and outreach. The award will be used to support the Atlanta Chapter for the Society for Neuroscience and the Fong Lab’s STEM outreach program for local blind and visually-impaired youth.
entry.summary: Ming received the 2025 Next Generation Award from the Society for Neuroscience in honor of her efforts in science education and outreach. The award will be used to support the Atlanta Chapter for the Society for Neuroscience and the Fong Lab’s STEM outreach program for local blind and visually-impaired youth.

entry.title: Welcome Eli :)
entry.content: We are happy to welcome Elijah Lapeza to the Fong Lab for a research rotation. Eli is a first-year PhD student in GT-Emory Biomedical Engineering Program and a fellow in the NIBIB T32 Computational Neural Engineering Training Program. Eli is interested in neural plasticity and rehabilitation, and joins the lab to work on projects related to how early life experiences shapes neural responses in the mature mammalian visual system. Welcome, Eli!
entry.summary: We are happy to welcome Elijah Lapeza to the Fong Lab for a research rotation. Eli is a first-year PhD student in GT-Emory Biomedical Engineering Program and a fellow in the NIBIB T32 Computational Neural Engineering Training Program. Eli is interested in neural plasticity and rehabilitation, and joins the lab to work on projects related to how early life experiences shapes neural responses in the mature mammalian visual system. Welcome, Eli!

entry.title: Danielle awarded AAUW International Fellowship
entry.content: Big news! Danielle Dowe has been awarded the prestigious AAUW International Fellowship for doctoral students. The fellowship will support her research on learning and homeostasis in biologically-plausible neural networks. We are thrilled to have an AAUW fellow in our ranks! Congratulations, Danielle!
entry.summary: Big news! Danielle Dowe has been awarded the prestigious AAUW International Fellowship for doctoral students. The fellowship will support her research on learning and homeostasis in biologically-plausible neural networks. We are thrilled to have an AAUW fellow in our ranks! Congratulations, Danielle!

entry.title: Welcome Sydney :)
entry.content: We are thrilled to welcome Sydney Gaines to the Fong Lab! Sydney is a high school student researcher in Project ENGAGES at Georgia Tech and is passionate about neuroscience and biotechnology. She brings a strong commitment to leadership and advocacy through her involvement in student organizations. In the lab, Sydney is excited to learn, grow as a researcher, and explore how neuroscience and technology can make a positive impact in healthcare. Welcome, Sydney!
entry.summary: We are thrilled to welcome Sydney Gaines to the Fong Lab! Sydney is a high school student researcher in Project ENGAGES at Georgia Tech and is passionate about neuroscience and biotechnology. She brings a strong commitment to leadership and advocacy through her involvement in student organizations. In the lab, Sydney is excited to learn, grow as a researcher, and explore how neuroscience and technology can make a positive impact in healthcare. Welcome, Sydney!

entry.title: Welcome Alissa :)
entry.content: Alissa Klein is the newest member of the Fong Lab! Alissa is an undergraduate student at Georgia Tech majoring in Biomedical Engineering and joins our lab through the Emory Ophthalmology SEE Intern Program. She will be conducting research in the lab with Morgan and Adam, while also shadowing clinical ophthalmologists at Emory. Welcome, Alissa!
entry.summary: Alissa Klein is the newest member of the Fong Lab! Alissa is an undergraduate student at Georgia Tech majoring in Biomedical Engineering and joins our lab through the Emory Ophthalmology SEE Intern Program. She will be conducting research in the lab with Morgan and Adam, while also shadowing clinical ophthalmologists at Emory. Welcome, Alissa!
